content.problemStatement:
**This is the easy version of the problem. Here, it is guaranteed that P2i=0P_{2i} = 0P2i​=0 for all iii.**

You are given a permutation†^\dagger† PPP of the integers 111 to 2N2N2N, with some of its elements missing (represented by 000's).

Find the number of ways of filling in the zeros to obtain a valid permutation, such that the quantity

∣P1−P2∣+∣P3−P4∣+…+∣P2N−1−P2N∣|P_1 - P_2| + |P_3 - P_4| + \ldots + |P_{2N-1} - P_{2N}|∣P1​−P2​∣+∣P3​−P4​∣+…+∣P2N−1​−P2N​∣is maximized.

**In this version of the problem, there is an additional constraint on the input:** all the elements at even positions are 000, that is,

P2=P4=P6=…=P2N=0P_2 = P_4 = P_6 = \ldots = P_{2N} = 0P2​=P4​=P6​=…=P2N​=0.

Note that the elements at odd positions may or may not be zero.

The answer can be large, so find it modulo 998244353998 244 353998244353.

†^\dagger† A permutation of the integers 111 to NNN is an array of length NNN that contains every integer from 111 to NNN exactly once.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — meaning that PPP has length 2N2N2N.
The second line contains 2N2N2N space-separated integers P1,P2,…,P2NP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_{2N}P1​,P2​,…,P2N​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of ways of filling in the zeros to maximize the sum, modulo 998244353998 244 353998244353.
constraints: All the non-zero elements of PPP are distinct.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.
